sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A sleep laboratory randomly assigned adults to one night of 8 hours, 6 hours, or 4 hours in bed. The next morning, participants completed a sustained-attention task. Mean vigilance lapses were 5, 11, and 24 across the three conditions. Mean omission errors were 2, 5, and 12. Participants in the 4-hour condition also reported the most microsleep episodes and the lowest alertness ratings.
questions.stem: Which sleep condition produced the most vigilance lapses?

choices.D: All conditions produced the same number

explanations.C: Correct. The 4-hour condition produced 24 lapses, which is more than double the 6-hour condition and nearly five times the 8-hour condition. The figure identifies restricted sleep as strongly associated with poorer sustained attention.
explanations.A: The 8-hour condition had the fewest lapses.
explanations.B: The 6-hour condition was intermediate rather than highest.
explanations.D: The lapse counts differ substantially across conditions.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Mean vigilance lapses and omission errors after three sleep-duration conditions.

questions.stem: Because sleep duration was randomly assigned, which conclusion is best supported?

choices.A: Sleep restriction likely contributed to poorer attention the next morning
choices.B: Participants chose sleep duration based on motivation
choices.C: The task measured only personality traits
choices.D: Sleep duration had no effect because all adults need sleep

explanations.A: Correct. Random assignment makes sleep duration the key systematic difference among groups, strengthening the conclusion that restricted sleep contributed to next-day attention problems in this experiment.
explanations.B: Participants did not choose duration; it was assigned by the researchers.
explanations.C: The task measured lapses and errors, not only personality.
explanations.D: The data show large differences across sleep conditions.

questions.stem: Microsleep episodes are most relevant because they indicate what?

choices.D: Brief intrusions of sleep-like states into wakefulness
choices.A: A permanent loss of memory encoding
choices.B: The complete absence of circadian rhythms

explanations.D: Correct. Microsleeps are short lapses in wakefulness that can occur during sleep deprivation. Their increase in the 4-hour group helps explain the vigilance failures and low alertness ratings.
explanations.A: The passage does not suggest permanent memory loss.
explanations.B: Circadian rhythms are not absent; the issue is insufficient sleep.
explanations.C: Social facilitation concerns performance in the presence of others, not sleep intrusions.

questions.stem: A hospital applying these results to safety-sensitive work would most likely do what?

choices.B: Limit severely sleep-restricted staff from tasks requiring sustained attention
choices.A: Increase 4-hour sleep schedules to build endurance
choices.C: Ignore omission errors if workers report confidence
choices.D: Assign the most restricted workers to the longest monitoring tasks

explanations.B: Correct. The experiment shows sharp increases in lapses and errors after only 4 hours in bed. In safety-sensitive settings, limiting high-attention tasks after severe sleep restriction would directly address the demonstrated risk.
explanations.A: More severe restriction would likely worsen the pattern.
explanations.C: Objective errors matter even if confidence is high.
explanations.D: Long monitoring tasks would be especially vulnerable to lapses.

questions.stem: Which result would most strongly support a dose-response relationship?

choices.C: Attention errors increase progressively from 8 to 6 to 4 hours of sleep
choices.B: Participants in all groups complete the task in the same room

explanations.C: Correct. A dose-response pattern means worsening outcomes as the exposure becomes more intense. The progressive increase in lapses and errors as sleep time decreases is exactly that pattern.
explanations.A: Alertness in one group alone would not establish a graded relationship.
explanations.B: A shared testing room standardizes context but does not show dose-response.
explanations.D: Nonparticipation would undermine rather than strengthen the pattern.

questions.passage: A consciousness seminar reviewed a hypnosis study in which participants received suggestions that a cold-water task would feel manageable and that they could focus attention away from discomfort. Highly hypnotizable participants reported lower pain ratings during the task than they had during a baseline trial. Participants low in hypnotizability showed smaller changes. The researchers argued that hypnosis is not sleep, but a state involving focused attention, expectation, and altered response to suggestion.
questions.stem: The researchers’ description of hypnosis most directly rejects which misconception?

choices.D: Hypnosis is the same as sleep

explanations.D: Correct. The passage explicitly states that hypnosis is not sleep. It instead describes hypnosis as involving focused attention, expectancy, and responsiveness to suggestion.
explanations.A: Focused attention is part of the researchers’ account rather than a misconception.
explanations.B: The study supports the idea that expectations influence pain ratings.
explanations.C: Variation in hypnotizability is directly described.

questions.stem: Why did highly hypnotizable participants show larger pain reductions?

choices.A: They were more responsive to suggestion and attentional focusing instructions
choices.D: They were randomly assigned to be more suggestible

explanations.A: Correct. The passage links the effect to hypnotizability, suggestion, and attention. Highly hypnotizable participants likely used the suggested focus more effectively, reducing the subjective pain experience.
explanations.B: The cold-water task was the same baseline-to-hypnosis comparison, not a reduced exposure.
explanations.C: Pain receptors are not absent in highly hypnotizable individuals.
explanations.D: Hypnotizability is an individual difference, not something randomly assigned in the passage.

questions.stem: The study best illustrates which broader idea about perception?

choices.B: Top-down processes can shape pain experience
choices.A: Pain is determined only by tissue damage
choices.C: Cold stimuli cannot be perceived differently
choices.D: Attention has no role in subjective experience

explanations.B: Correct. Suggestions and focused attention altered pain ratings even though the physical task remained cold water. This demonstrates that beliefs and attention can modulate perception.
explanations.A: The study contradicts a purely tissue-damage account of pain.
explanations.C: Participants did perceive the stimulus differently across conditions.
explanations.D: Attention is central to the mechanism described.

questions.stem: Which clinical use would be most consistent with the passage?

choices.C: Using hypnosis as an adjunct for selected patients who are responsive to suggestion
choices.A: Replacing all anesthesia with hypnosis for every patient
choices.B: Using hypnosis only by telling patients they are asleep
choices.D: Assuming low-hypnotizability patients will show the largest effects

explanations.C: Correct. The results varied by hypnotizability, so the most cautious application is as an adjunct for patients likely to respond. It should not be treated as a universal substitute for medical pain control.
explanations.A: The passage does not support replacing medical anesthesia for everyone.
explanations.B: The researchers specifically reject the idea that hypnosis is sleep.
explanations.D: Low-hypnotizability participants showed smaller changes, not larger ones.

questions.stem: Which limitation should be considered when interpreting the study?

choices.A: Subjective pain ratings can be influenced by demand characteristics as well as actual experience
choices.B: Cold-water tasks cannot be used in research
choices.D: Baseline trials make the study invalid

explanations.A: Correct. Pain ratings are meaningful, but participants may also respond to what they think the experimenter expects. A careful interpretation considers both genuine perceptual modulation and possible demand characteristics.
explanations.B: Cold-water tasks are commonly used as controlled pain stimuli.
explanations.C: The passage explicitly states that people vary in hypnotizability.
explanations.D: Baseline trials help compare change within participants rather than invalidate the study.
